topic: PLG is dead. Long live Sales-Led Growth.

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Welcome to The Arena, I am so pumped you're here for this one. Elena Verna has literally built PLG engines at Miro, SurveyMonkey, and Amplitude, so if anyone can defend this model it is her. Jason Lemkin founded SaaStr and has spent fifteen years watching what actually closes deals at scale, which gives him a very different read on reality. The question today is simple but brutal: is PLG a genuine growth motion or was it a zero-interest-rate fantasy that sales will now quietly bury? Elena, I'll start with you — make the case. Why should anyone still bet on product-led growth in 2025?

turns.speaker: Elena Verna
turns.text:
Let me be direct: this framing is lazy, and the business world deserves better than pendulum thinking.

PLG is not dead. What's dead is the naive version of PLG that founders and investors mistook for a complete go-to-market strategy — the belief that if you just make a great product and let users self-serve, growth will compound forever with no sales motion required. That version deserved to die. But conflating the failure of *pure PLG* with the death of product-led principles is a category error. When I was at SurveyMonkey and later advising growth-stage companies, the core insight behind PLG — that your product is your most efficient and scalable acquisition and retention channel — hasn't changed. What's changed is the macro environment, which has made every motion more expensive, and the talent market, which oversold PLG as a silver bullet.

Here's the framework I actually use: PLG and Sales-Led Growth are not opposites on a spectrum. They are *layers* in a go-to-market stack. The real question companies need to ask is sequencing and fit — what does your user acquisition loop look like, where does value get realized in the product, and at what point does human intervention accelerate or unlock revenue that the product alone cannot close? This is why the smarter conversation is about PLG-assisted sales, or what many are now calling Product-Led Sales. The companies abandoning PLG entirely aren't making a strategic pivot — they're overcorrecting because they implemented PLG incorrectly in the first place. They skipped the hard work of building genuine product virality and retention, slapped "PLG" on a free trial, and then blamed the motion when CAC didn't drop.

So here's my challenge to anyone celebrating the return of Sales-Led Growth: show me a purely sales-led company at scale that isn't desperately trying to build more product-led signals into their pipeline right now. Gong, Salesforce, Workday — they're all investing in product usage data to make their sales teams smarter. The direction of travel is *toward* the product being central to growth, not away from it. So who exactly declared PLG dead, and what are they selling you instead?

turns.speaker: Jason M. Lemkin
turns.text:
Let me be direct: this framing is wrong, and frankly, it's the kind of lazy binary thinking that gets founders into real trouble.

PLG isn't dead. What's dead is the *fantasy version* of PLG — the idea that you can just build a great product, add a free tier, watch the usage metrics climb, and somehow magically convert that into a $100M ARR business without ever having a serious sales motion. That version? Yes, that's dead. Good riddance. But the baby is getting thrown out with the bathwater here, and I think it's genuinely dangerous advice for founders who are now over-correcting.

As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, one of the core mistakes PLG companies make is putting too much revenue pressure directly on the product team — essentially treating your growth and product function like a quota-carrying sales rep. That breaks something important. The product-led motion works when you protect the integrity of the free experience, when someone — what I call the "VP of Free" or really the head of growth — is obsessively focused on the self-serve flywheel *separate* from the enterprise sales pressure. When you collapse that, when you tell your product team "your OKR is revenue," you don't get better sales-led growth. You get a worse product AND worse sales results. I've seen this pattern repeat across companies.

Here's the challenge I'd put to anyone waving the "Sales-Led" banner right now: show me a durable, scalable SaaS business at $50M+ ARR that has *zero* product-led motion — no self-serve, no freemium, no bottoms-up adoption — and tell me what their CAC payback period looks like. The best companies aren't choosing between PLG and sales-led. They're figuring out how to layer them intelligently. So what's the actual evidence that abandoning the product-led motion produces *better* unit economics? I'd love to see it.

sources.title: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened | Jason Lemkin (SaaStr)
sources.snippet: I probably should have clarified. I think this is mostly true in PLG companies where most of the growth is coming from product, but I imagin

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, I love that you both basically agreed with each other in your opening statements, which is a little suspicious. You both said "the fantasy version of PLG is dead" — but that's a cop-out. Every failed strategy looks naive in hindsight.

So Jason, here's what I actually want to know: name a company that did PLG right, with real discipline, and still hit a wall that only sales could break through. Give me a concrete example, not a category.

turns.speaker: Elena Verna
turns.text:
Jason, I agree with more of this than I expected to — and that actually sharpens my point, not dulls it.

The "VP of Free" framing is real and important. When I was at SurveyMonkey and later at Miro, one of the consistent failure modes I watched unfold — both internally and at peer companies — was exactly what you described: collapsing the product-led motion into a revenue accountability structure designed for sales. The moment your free tier becomes a negotiating chip in enterprise deals rather than a genuine acquisition engine, you've broken the flywheel. We're not disagreeing there.

But here's where I want to push back on the broader narrative fueling this debate: the "Sales-Led Growth is back" crowd isn't just arguing for better integration — they're often arguing for *abandonment*. And the companies I watch doing that are not winning on unit economics, they're just deferring the reckoning. Sales-led without a product-led foundation means your CAC scales linearly with your headcount, your retention depends on relationship management rather than product value, and your expansion revenue requires a QBR instead of a usage signal. When I developed my thinking around growth loops versus funnels — the core idea that compound growth requires a re-entry mechanism — that's exactly what PLG provides that a pure sales motion structurally cannot. Sales closes deals. Product creates the conditions for deals to close themselves.

So here's my challenge to the room: if the real enemy is bad PLG execution rather than PLG itself, why are we calling it a eulogy? What specific failure in product-led execution are founders actually looking at right now that sales headcount genuinely solves — and not just defers?

turns.speaker: Jason M. Lemkin
turns.text:
Elena, you're right that a lot of the "Sales-Led is back" crowd is arguing for abandonment, and that's genuinely bad advice. I've watched founders rip out their self-serve motion the moment an enterprise deal required a custom contract, and they regret it within 18 months when their pipeline dries up and their net revenue retention drops below 100%. So yes, I'm not arguing for abandonment.

But here's where I want to sharpen the real tension, because I think you're letting PLG off the hook a little too easily. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the failure mode I keep seeing isn't just "bad execution" — it's that in PLG companies, *nobody stands up for the free user*. Everyone's optimizing the tail, monetizing the signal, converting the power users. Meanwhile the 590,000 people who are never going to pay but would become your best champions, your word-of-mouth engine, your actual flywheel — they're completely orphaned. That's not a sales problem. That's a structural accountability problem inside PLG organizations. The growth loop you're describing only compounds if someone is genuinely stewarding the top of it, and most PLG companies don't have that person with real authority.

So here's the challenge I'd put back to you: the companies that are genuinely winning right now — I'm thinking about the ones in the SaaStr orbit doing $50M to $200M ARR with real efficiency — most of them have a *hybrid* motion where sales isn't just closing deals, it's actually feeding product insight back into the loop. The question isn't PLG versus Sales-Led. The question is whether your PLG motion has enough accountability infrastructure to actually sustain the compounding you're describing — or whether it's just a beautiful theory that works great at Miro's scale and breaks down for the other 95% of companies trying to copy it without the brand, the category leadership, or the patience of your investors.

sources.title: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened | Jason Lemkin (SaaStr)
sources.snippet: I probably should have clarified. I think this is mostly true in PLG companies where most of the growth is coming from product, but I imagin

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Here's my actual hot take that neither of you has touched: the real killer of PLG isn't sales, it's AI.

When your product's core value can be replicated by a ChatGPT wrapper in a weekend, the moat you thought you had — the one that was supposed to make users invite colleagues and spread virally — evaporates overnight. I'll admit I used to think "build a great product and distribution follows," but I'm genuinely less sure of that now.

So here's the spicy version of the question: if your PLG flywheel depends on product stickiness, and AI is commoditizing product differentiation faster than any of us expected, does the whole debate between PLG and sales-led actually become irrelevant? Because the real question might be — what's the moat, period?

turns.speaker: Elena Verna
turns.text:
Jason, you're landing on something real with the "nobody stands up for the free user" problem — and I'll give you full credit for naming it clearly. But I'd push back on the framing that this is a structural indictment of PLG as a model. What you're describing is a *prioritization failure*, and prioritization failures happen in every go-to-market motion. Sales-led companies orphan their mid-market segment all the time — everyone's chasing enterprise logos, and the $15K ACV customer gets a boilerplate sequence and a junior rep who's gone in six months. The failure mode isn't unique to PLG; it's what happens when you don't build explicit accountability around the segments that don't scream loudest in your revenue dashboard.

Here's where I'll genuinely extend your argument though: the accountability infrastructure problem is real, and it's underbuilt in most PLG organizations. When I was at SurveyMonkey, the tension wasn't between product and sales — it was between who owned the *activation loop* at the top versus who owned the *expansion loop* at the bottom. Those are different jobs requiring different incentives, different metrics, and different leadership mandates. Most PLG companies try to collapse them into one "growth" function and wonder why neither compounds properly. You need someone whose sole job is making the free experience so valuable that it creates genuine pull — not just enough friction removal to nudge someone toward a paywall. That person rarely exists with real authority, and you're right that this is where the flywheel quietly stalls.

But here's where I'll push back on your $50M-$200M hybrid framing: the companies you're pointing to that are "winning with hybrid" — I'd want to know how much of their efficiency is coming *from* the PLG foundation versus *despite* the sales overlay they added. Because what I keep seeing is founders who built a real product-led engine, then added sales and credited sales for the acceleration, when actually they were just harvesting what PLG had already built. The question isn't whether hybrid wins — of course it does, I've never argued otherwise. The question is which motion is actually doing the compounding work, and whether the sales layer is genuinely feeding insight back into product or just taking credit for organic demand. So tell me: in the companies you're watching at SaaStr, how many can actually trace a closed enterprise deal back to product insight that changed the self-serve motion? Because that's the real test of whether it's a flywheel or just a funnel with two separate engines running in parallel.

turns.speaker: Jason M. Lemkin
turns.text:
Elena, you're making a sharp distinction between activation loops and expansion loops — and honestly, that framework is more precise than how most founders think about it. You're right that collapsing those into one undifferentiated "growth" function is where a lot of PLG companies quietly lose the thread. I'll take that as a genuine extension of what I've been arguing.

But here's where I'm going to push back on your question about whether the sales layer is "harvesting PLG" or actually doing compounding work. I think you're setting up a false test. At the SaaStr companies I work with that are doing $50M-$200M ARR with hybrid motions — and I see hundreds of these across the portfolio and community — the insight rarely flows cleanly from sales back to product in some traceable, documented way. That's not how it works in practice. What actually happens is that the enterprise deals surface *use cases* the PLG motion never would have discovered, because free users self-select into the easy jobs-to-be-done. The sales team is sitting in rooms — or Zooms — hearing about workflow problems that no free user ever articulates in a product analytics dashboard. That's real product insight. It's just not formatted as a feedback loop that shows up in your activation metrics.

As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the real risk I worry about isn't which motion gets credit — it's who stands up for the 590,000 users who will never convert. And here's my challenge to you: in every company where you've seen the activation loop and expansion loop properly separated with distinct leadership and incentives — which is the right model, I agree — did the person running activation actually have *veto power* when the monetization team wanted to add friction? Because without that authority, the structure is just org chart theater. The flywheel only compounds if the free experience is genuinely protected, not just nominally represented.

sources.title: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened | Jason Lemkin (SaaStr)
sources.snippet: This idea of VP of free, I think technically it's probably the head of growth for a PLG company or the person leading the self-serve part of

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, let me try to land this plane before we spiral into another three rounds of "we actually agree more than we disagree."

Here's what I'm hearing as genuine consensus: PLG as a viral magic trick is dead, free users need an actual owner, and the activation loop and expansion loop are fundamentally different problems that can't live in the same function. You both signed off on all of that.

Here's where you still actually differ: Jason thinks the ceiling on pure PLG is structural — that at some point enterprise complexity forces a human into the loop and no amount of product elegance gets around that. Elena thinks that ceiling is mostly a symptom of bad prioritization and cowardly leadership, not an indictment of the model itself. That's a real disagreement, and I don't think either of you fully resolved it.

And here's the question I think is still hanging in the air, which is the one I actually want answered

turns.speaker: Elena Verna
turns.text:
Jason, the veto power question is the sharpest thing you've said in this entire conversation, and I want to sit with it for a second before pushing back. You're right that org chart structure without authority is theater. At SurveyMonkey, when I was running growth, the activation function had influence but not veto power — and what that meant in practice was that monetization decisions got made in rooms where nobody was explicitly accountable for the free user experience. The workaround was making the free user metrics *visible at the executive level*, so killing the free experience had a cost that showed up on a dashboard the CEO cared about. That's not the same as veto power, but it created enough friction to slow down bad decisions. So yes — authority matters, but authority can be constructed through metric visibility and executive accountability, not just org hierarchy.

On your point about enterprise deals surfacing use cases that PLG never discovers — I actually agree with the observation and dispute the conclusion. Yes, sales teams hear workflow problems in enterprise Zooms that no free user articulates in a Mixpanel dashboard. That's real. But the question is what happens next. In most hybrid companies I've seen, that insight goes into a sales playbook, not a product roadmap. The discovery is real; the feedback loop is broken. The insight compounds *for sales*, not *for product*, which means you're not actually building a better flywheel — you're building a better sales process. Those are genuinely different outcomes. If the enterprise insight were systematically flowing back into the activation layer, you'd see PLG conversion rates improving over time in hybrid companies. That's a testable claim. Do you actually see that in your SaaStr portfolio?

Here's where I'll leave the challenge: the companies that get this right — and there are some — have a specific person whose job it is to close the loop between what sales hears and what the product team builds for the free experience. Not a "voice of customer" program, not a quarterly business review summary, but someone whose *compensation* depends on free-to-paid conversion improving as a result of enterprise learnings. That role almost never exists. And until it does, the hybrid motion isn't a flywheel — it's two parallel revenue machines that occasionally wave at each other across the org chart.

turns.speaker: Jason M. Lemkin
turns.text:
Elena, the metric visibility point is genuinely smart, and I've seen it work in practice — when free user health shows up on the CEO's weekly dashboard next to ARR, it creates organizational gravity that's almost as good as formal authority. Almost. The problem is that dashboards get deprioritized when enterprise revenue is accelerating. You've seen this too — when net new ARR from enterprise is up 40%, nobody's asking hard questions about free user activation rates in the all-hands. So metric visibility is a real mechanism, but it's a mechanism with a pressure valve, and that valve opens exactly when you need the protection most.

On your testable claim about whether enterprise insights are actually improving PLG conversion rates in hybrid companies — you're right to frame it that way, and honestly, in most of the SaaStr portfolio I've observed, the answer is no, it's not systematic. You'll see spikes when a thoughtful product leader happens to be in the right enterprise call, but that's a person, not a process. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the real problem is that nobody is explicitly accountable for the free user experience — and what you're now adding to that is that nobody is accountable for the *translation layer* between enterprise discovery and free product improvement. That's a more precise diagnosis than I had, and it matters.

Here's my challenge back to you though: you're describing a role that almost never exists, and I agree with that observation — but I'd argue the reason it doesn't exist is that PLG companies are systematically undervaluing the enterprise signal, not that they can't build the role. The companies I've watched actually close this loop — and there are a handful — did it by making the Head of Growth co-own a specific metric tied to enterprise-sourced feature adoption in free accounts. That's not a "voice of customer" program, that's compensation alignment. So the question isn't whether the role can exist — it's whether PLG leaders are willing to give up the narrative that the product speaks for itself, and actually do the unglamorous work of mining sales calls for activation insights. Are PLG purists actually willing to do that, or does it feel like a concession that breaks the whole ideology?
